Hey, what's going on everyone? I want to talk to you about the fifth habit, which is managing relationships. And there's obviously lots of relationships that you have in your life. You've got your employee relationships, you have your marriage relationships, you have your business partnerships relationships, lots and lots of dynamics. And so I'm going to try and cover them as briefly as I can in terms of overarching principles and then tactics. So this is kind of from a social aspect. Normal people will not like you. And that is because you are doing things that make them feel self-conscious. You have freedom, you took risks to start your own business and they want that but they aren't willing to take the risk. And so on a subconscious level, a lot of them will not root for you. And so when you say like, I don't want to do the things that you're doing, they will take that as a judgment on you judging them for their lifestyle, which on some level they know is true, but they don't want to accept that, they don't want to confront it. So then instead they basically decrease you in their mind. So normal people will not like you. Sometimes your family will stop liking how you do things because it's so different from what they do. And a lot of people have this. Like most of their, you know, it happens. And the point is like we can't expect them to be okay with it because we're doing something that's 100% opposite of their lives, which disrupts their values. And a lot of times highlights the deficiencies that they have and they're not comfortable with it. To further that point, understand that your goals aren't normal. So your life won't be normal in the eyes of most people. And so you have to accept that. And so sometimes that means having smaller circles, you know, it means, you know, having, you know, having smaller circles of friends and being okay with that. And sometimes having periods of time where you don't have tons of friends because you're just so focused on achieving what you want to achieve. I'm not saying you can't be social. That's not my point here. I'm just saying if it does happen, don't think you're doing something wrong. I guess I'm here to tell you like you're not normal, but among the group of people that you that are high achievers, it is normal, but it's just not normal for everyone else. Okay, business partnerships and dynamics. This is just the theory based. The best presentation you can possibly watch is the visionary integrator dynamic, the first follower presentation Laila and I gave in the January summit. It should be linked underneath of here. It goes into tons of detail about this, but essentially in your partnership, both of you cannot have the same skillset. If you're both getting marketing, both good at sales, both like training, one of you is not necessary. And a lot of people hear me talk against business partnerships a lot and I feel strongly about it because I've had seven failed partnerships. And a lot of times it's because I did not understand this is that like just because you're a friend, this does not mean you're going to be a good partnership. There's really almost no reason to be in a partnership unless someone provided you money that you did not have to start. That's basically one of the only reasons to let that happen. If that is not the case, the secondary scenario would be you have complementary skillsets, meaning one of you is process based, detail based, likes managing operations and the other one, other person likes managing the promotion, right? Which is usually you have one person who's the more visionary, innovator, sales, marketing, always breaking things and changing stuff. You need one person who's the breaks, who's consistent and operates and runs the gym and that component of it. If you are in a partnership and those roles are not clear, you need to make them clear, which I'll get to in the tactics, but like you have to have both of those roles within any business. That's why there's CEOs and COOs. It's the very basic level and this is just on a smaller scale, but it functions the exact same way. One of you is casting the vision of what you want to have happen. The other one is helping that happen. That's why it's the visionary, integrator dynamic or it's the first follower dynamic. The person who's your biggest fan is always rooted for you. Sometimes that's your spouse, sometimes it's not. Marriage dynamics. This is one that we covered in our most recent summit because it was kind of the, you know, the next sequential topic that would come up is like, okay, well, that's how we do business partnerships, but how do we do marriage? We've only seen marriage dynamics work within an entrepreneurial relationship in two ways. One is fullback, one is cheerleader. Fullback is where someone is in there with you. Layla and I would be a fullback relationship. We're both in the thick of it, elbow deep, dirt under a fingernail, working to try and win the game. The cheerleader dynamic is someone who is cheering for you from the sidelines. They're at home holding the fort down, making sure that they got all that covered so that you can go play the game. Okay? A cheerleader would never tell the quarterback to sit out from the game unless they were hurt. All right? So if you have a cheerleader who's trying to get you off the field and you're not hurt, that's not a cheerleader. And so you need to confront those dynamics. If you feel like you're not in either of them, then you need to figure out which one you are and go all in on that. And so you just can't have gray area. Well, like they're sort of in support of me. It's like, no, like either you need to be all in and like come in with me and like, let's do this. Or you need to be all in on rooting for me because I need your support. Right? And if you don't have that, that's a conversation you need to have and get that out of the tactics, which I'll be to right now. So how do you do this from a tactical standpoint? I should have changed this tactic. Sorry about that. First thing you do is you audit your friend group. So if you've got people who are around you, who are not positive, who are not contributing, who are not speaking positivity into your life, or speaking things over you that are speaking things of your past about your future, like, well, you know, that's just not who you are. Like they start saying stuff like that, like get those people out of your life. Okay? Like get them out of your life. They're not going to help you get to where you want to go and you need to be okay with that. All right? It's hard decisions. Like all this stuff's hard. It's hard because there's relationships. There's emotions. There's past. There's histories. But like if you want to get to where you want to go, it's not going to be how you got here. All right? So audit your group and be very real with yourself about like, is this person really someone who's going to help me get to where I want to go? That includes family. All right? Next, be comfortable with a smaller group. I said this earlier, but like when you start pruning the tree, the group shrinks, but I, you know, you should rather have one to two really good relationships. And that's what this community is so great for. It's like, we talked about their entrepreneurs for doing the same thing. Like some of the best friendships I've seen have been formed in this community because we're all trying to do the same thing. We have the same values. We're trying to go to the same place. And a lot of times you can't find that outside of this. So be comfortable with a smaller group. It's okay. Like it's normal. Avoid complainers and don't be one. Cut those people out of your life and definitely don't be one because that means you're a victim and you're not taking responsibility. Now with marriage roles, you need to discuss the roles and the goals. So goals being like, what do we want from our life together? What is our mutual goal that we want to both do both accomplish? All right? So you start with the goal and then what are our roles that will help us go there? All right? And then you have to figure out whether you're a cheerleader or fullback dynamic. You have to figure that out. And this is like sometimes it takes multiple discussions, but you have to be unbelievably bluntly truthful with one another about what you want. And if the goals are aligned, then you need to figure out how you can't align them because if your goals are aligned, there's big problems. All right? Partnership roles. Okay? Here's the same thing is that you need to write down all the activities of the business that you guys do on a regular basis. Anything that you both do, one of you needs to pick. All right? And then once you have delineated who's doing stuff, which is usually going to be more operational, running the team, running the day to day, the business, etc. And then the other person is going to be doing more of the promotion, more of the sales, more of the innovation, creating new programs, all that kind of stuff. Typically that's how it breaks up. It's not always that way, but that's typically where you'll see the break in personality types. If both of you like the same stuff, cool. Layla and I both liked sales, both liked marketing, but she took over building the culture, building the team, leading the company. And that was her swim lane. And she doesn't come into my swim lane about how I do marketing, how I do sales. I don't wonder hers about how she runs the culture, how she runs the team. We respect each other and we bounce ideas off one another because we respect each other's opinion. But the ultimate decision lies in the camp of the person who those roles and actions go under. Okay? So figure out all the things that you, like write down all the things that you guys do if you have a partnership and then put them each under one of you, not both of you. There's not like both of you shouldn't do anything. Your roles. And then that way, if everyone owns everything, then no one owns anything. Yeah, you know what I'm saying. If everyone owns it, then no one does. And so that's why you have to have that because then you can have accountability to execute it. All right? So figure out the roles, create them and then live by them. And then this is the wish list, which is something that we brought up on our first marriage talk. We brought it up during both of our presentations about the business partnership and the marriage one. And I think it's one of the most powerful things you can possibly do in your most significant relationships, which is a wish list for what you would like from that person. And you have to be truly selfish in how you're thinking about this. And I'm saying selfish is like what you need, not what you think you should need or what you think you should want, but what you truly desire. And most times insecurities that we have come out. Okay? So if like you want your, and this goes for a partnership like with your business partner and it goes for your marriage as well. It's both because it's just how you manage your relationship. If you want that person to compliment you on every piece of content you make because it means something to you, then you need to say that. Right? And a lot of times what happens is we start resenting someone for addicts, for expectations that we had never communicated. All right? And so this is your chance to truly communicate everything that you desire in your deepest heart. Okay? So the reason that affairs happen or that you fall out of love with your business partner and I'm like, I'm using that lightly is that someone starts fulfilling a need that you did not articulate to your partner. Right? And so they start doing something that you have been craving but never said. And so you have to give your partner, either your business partner or your marriage partner the opportunity to win. You have to give them the cheat codes. And I can tell you when you actually do this and you sit down and you write out these things, it will change your life. It changed my marriage forever and I want this for you, genuinely. Like it changed my life. And as soon as Layla told me everything that she wanted me to do, I was like, I love you. This is really what you want me to do. And she was like, yes, if you just do that, I'll be stoked. I'll be like, awesome. I'll just do that. And so you'll see that you had all this effort that you were putting in one direction that was wasted because they didn't really care about that. And they really just care about these things. And so you just execute those things and the same thing goes to them. And you execute independent of how they're executing because you take full responsibility because you are going to do your part. And you're going to make everything on your part to make their part easier because you take full responsibility. And then lo and behold, when you start doing your side, even if they're not doing their side, they'll start doing their side. Guaranteed. That's how it works. That's a process. Okay? That covers relationship theory and tactics. The in-depth talks about the visionary integrator dynamic. You can watch that video. And the same thing for the fullback versus cheerleader marriage dynamic. You can watch those videos with your VISTA partner and your spouse. They will be incredibly enlightening for you. And I think almost every summit, that's one of the favorite presentations that we get where people make huge decisions and it changes their lives in a good way.
